question: We're evaluating Algolia vs building search in-house on Elasticsearch — why Algolia for a B2C retail catalog?
answer:
You know that feeling when you're trying to find that one specific item in a massive warehouse, and it feels like you're digging through a haystack for a needle? That's what building search in-house on Elasticsearch can feel like, especially when you're trying to scale. We make that needle find itself.

When you're looking at a B2C retail catalog, the stakes are sky-high for **search relevance** and **user experience**. Shoppers who use site search are a goldmine; they spend **2.6x more** than non-searchers and drive a significant chunk of **ecommerce revenue** [1]. On the flip side, irrelevant results or a clunky search experience can send them running for the hills, leaving money on the table.

Here's why Algolia is the clear winner for a B2C retail catalog over a DIY Elasticsearch approach:

* **Blazing Fast Relevance & Speed:** Algolia is engineered for speed and relevance out-of-the-box. While Elasticsearch is powerful, tuning it for that lightning-fast, hyper-relevant search experience that keeps B2C shoppers engaged requires a ton of specialized expertise and ongoing effort. For instance, leading retailers like [Under Armour](https://www.algolia.com/customers/under-armour) saw a **35% increase in conversion rates** by leveraging Algolia's AI-powered search to deliver exactly what their customers were looking for, instantly.
* **Out-of-the-Box B2C Features:** We've built features specifically for the B2C retail world that are complex and time-consuming to replicate in-house. This includes things like:
 * **Personalization:** Tailoring search results based on individual user behavior and preferences to drive discovery and conversions.
 * **Faceted Search & Filtering:** Allowing customers to easily narrow down their options with intuitive filters that mirror how shoppers think.
 * **Typo Tolerance & Query Understanding:** Ensuring that misspellings or natural language queries still lead to the right products.
* **Reduced Operational Overhead:** Building and maintaining a robust search infrastructure on Elasticsearch is a significant undertaking. It requires dedicated engineering resources for tuning, scaling, and troubleshooting. With Algolia, you offload that complexity and operational burden, freeing up your team to focus on product and business strategy, not search infrastructure. This means you can deliver a world-class search experience without the headaches of managing a complex distributed system.
* **Faster Time to Value:** Getting a high-performing search experience up and running with Algolia is significantly faster than building and optimizing it on Elasticsearch from scratch. This allows you to start capturing the revenue uplift from improved search much sooner.

Think about it: while Elasticsearch provides the raw building blocks, Algolia provides a complete, optimized solution designed to drive **conversions** and enhance the **customer journey** in a B2C retail environment.

What is your role in evaluating search solutions for your B2C retail catalog?

question: What measurable business results do companies see from improving site search?
answer:
You know how sometimes you search for something online and it's like finding a needle in a haystack, but then other times it's like the internet just *knows* what you want? Yeah, that's the difference between a frustrating experience and a revenue-generating one. We aim for the latter.

## Driving Tangible Business Outcomes with Enhanced Site Search

Companies that supercharge their site search aren't just making it easier for users to find things; they're directly impacting their bottom line. It's all about turning that search bar into a powerful engine for growth.

Here's a look at the measurable business results companies are seeing:

* **Skyrocketing Conversion Rates:** This is the big one. When users can find what they're looking for quickly and efficiently, they're far more likely to buy. Studies show that site searchers are **2-3x more likely to convert** than those who don't use search [1, 2, 3]. Some companies have even seen conversion rates **nearly double** just by optimizing their on-site search [1, 2, 3]. For example, Amazon sees its conversion rate shoot up **6x** when visitors use search [1, 2, 3].
* **Increased Revenue:** Higher conversion rates naturally lead to more revenue. Retailers with advanced search capabilities see desktop conversion rates that are **2x higher** compared to those with basic search [1, 2, 3]. In fact, 50% of retailers report an **increase in revenue** directly from site search technology, with that number jumping to 93% for companies with advanced solutions [1, 2, 3].
* **Boosted Average Order Value:** It's not just about more transactions; it's about bigger ones too. Searchers tend to spend more, being **2.6x more likely to drive revenue** across mobile and desktop compared to non-searchers [1, 2, 3].
* **Reduced Bounce Rates and Increased Engagement:** Frustrating search experiences drive customers away. A staggering **68%** of users abandon a site due to poor product search experiences [1, 2, 3]. Conversely, when search works, users stick around. One company saw goal completions increase by **242%** by simply making their search bar more visible [10, 14].
* **Lower Support Costs:** When customers can find answers to their questions or locate products themselves through effective site search, they're less likely to contact support. This leads to a significant reduction in support tickets and associated costs [10, 14].
* **Enhanced Customer Loyalty:** A seamless, relevant search experience creates happy customers. When users feel understood and can easily find what they need, they're more likely to return and remain loyal to your brand [10, 14].

These aren't just theoretical benefits; they're concrete, measurable impacts that directly contribute to a healthier bottom line.

What industry are you in, and what kind of business results are you hoping to achieve?

question: How does Algolia help with merchandising and personalization for a fashion brand?
answer:
You know how some fashion brands make you feel like you're a VIP, and others make you feel like you're just another face in the crowd? Algolia is the secret sauce that turns every shopper into that VIP.

## Elevating Fashion Merchandising with AI

For fashion brands, merchandising isn't just about showing products; it's about telling a story and curating an experience that mirrors the exclusivity and flair of a high-end boutique. Algolia empowers fashion brands to do just that by bringing the sophistication of in-store curation to the digital realm.

* **Dynamic Collections for Curated Experiences:** Forget static category pages. With Algolia Collections, merchandisers can create dynamic, hand-picked assortments around specific themes, like a "Summer Wedding Guest Edit" or a "Coastal Grandmother Aesthetic." This allows for editorial precision that goes beyond broad filters, letting brands showcase perfect head-to-toe looks or themed collections without developer intervention [9].
* **AI-Powered Discovery:** Shoppers expect more than just keyword matches. Algolia's AI understands nuanced fashion language—think "quiet luxury" or "Gorpcore"—and continuously learns from real-time trends and brand-specific vocabulary. This means search and discovery feel intuitive and on-brand, even for evolving tastes [2].
* **Real-time Relevance:** Trends in fashion move at lightning speed. Algolia ensures your digital storefront stays ahead of the curve by refreshing results in real-time, highlighting what's new, relevant, and seasonally appropriate, and adapting to changing inventory instantly [2].

## Hyper-Personalization for the Fashion Consumer

Personalization in fashion is crucial because shopping is often an emotional and sensory experience. Algolia helps brands connect with shoppers on a deeper level by delivering tailored experiences at scale.

* **AI-Driven Outfit Completion & Recommendations:** Imagine a shopper adding a dress to their cart, and the AI suggesting the perfect shoes and accessories to complete the look, based on their style and browsing history. Algolia's AI-powered outfit completion and product recommendations go beyond simple "similar items" to truly understand shopper intent and preferences, which can [shorten time-to-checkout by up to 50%](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ecommerce/how-algolia-gives-digital-fashion-its-stylist-touch) [1].
* **Understanding Nuanced Intent:** Shoppers don't always know the exact terms to describe what they're looking for. Algolia's AI, powered by fashion-tuned LLMs and vector understanding, can interpret vague or descriptive queries to deliver highly relevant results, making it feel like an AI personal shopper is guiding them [2].
* **Brand Consistency:** A significant challenge for fashion brands is maintaining brand identity. Algolia grounds its AI in your specific product catalog and brand content, ensuring that AI responses are accurate, on-brand, and never "hallucinate" off-brand suggestions [2]. This is critical for high-fashion and luxury brands where brand perception is paramount.

Ultimately, Algolia helps fashion brands transform their online presence from a digital catalog into a personalized, curated shopping experience that drives engagement and conversion. For example, [Huckberry](https://www.algolia.com/customers/huckberry), a men's lifestyle retailer, saw a [9.4% increase in website revenue](https://www.algolia.com/customers/huckberry/) directly related to AI-driven personalization.

Are you a merchandiser, a developer, or in another role evaluating Algolia's capabilities for your fashion brand?

question: Doctors search 'T2DM' or 'heart attack' but our system only matches the full term — how does Algolia handle synonyms and natural-language understanding for specialized terminology?
answer:
You know when you're trying to explain something to your parents and they just don't get it? That's what bad search feels like for your users. We make sure your system speaks their language, even the super-technical stuff.

## Smarter Search for Specialized Terms

Doctors searching for 'T2DM' or 'heart attack' expect your system to understand they mean 'Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus' or 'myocardial infarction', respectively. Relying on exact term matches is like speaking a foreign language to your users – it just doesn't work. Algolia tackles this head-on with robust **synonym management** and **natural language understanding** capabilities.

We go beyond simple keyword matching to truly grasp user intent. This means:

* **Dynamic Synonyms:** Our system can automatically detect and adapt to synonyms based on context and user interactions. For instance, if users frequently search for 'heart attack' and click on results for 'myocardial infarction,' the system learns this connection. This is crucial for specialized fields where terminology can vary [as detailed in our blog post](https://algolia.com/blog/product/dynamic-synonyms-explanation/).
* **Contextual Understanding:** Algolia understands that a word can have different meanings. A search for 'mouse' on an electronics site should yield a computer accessory, while on a pet supply site, it should bring up a pet toy. This contextual awareness is vital for specialized medical or technical terms where precision is key.
* **Semantic Search Capabilities:** By leveraging techniques like Natural Language Processing (NLP) and vector search, Algolia can interpret the *meaning* behind queries, not just the words themselves. This allows it to connect 'T2DM' with 'Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus' even if the full term isn't explicitly typed.

For specialized terminology, this translates directly to **increased relevance** and **reduced frustration** for your users. Instead of missing critical information because they used a shorthand or a synonym, they get accurate results every time. This capability is a game-changer for industries where precise understanding is paramount, like healthcare or complex B2B environments.

question: What's the difference between Algolia's keyword search and NeuralSearch?
answer:
You know how some search engines feel like they're just guessing, and others *actually* get what you mean? That's the difference between a dusty Rolodex and a mind-reading psychic. Keyword search is the Rolodex, and NeuralSearch? That's the psychic.

## Keyword Search vs. NeuralSearch: The Core Difference

Think of traditional **keyword search** like looking up a word in a book's index. It’s all about exact matches. If you type "sneakers," it looks for documents that *explicitly* contain "sneakers." It’s functional, but it breaks down fast when users don't use the exact terms or when the meaning is more nuanced. This often leads to a frustrating experience where users can't find what they're looking for, leaving money on the table. We've seen this time and again with clients struggling with the "long tail" of search queries, where common keyword approaches just fall flat [12, 28].

**Algolia NeuralSearch**, on the other hand, goes way beyond simple keyword matching. It's built on **AI** and **vector search** principles, understanding the *meaning* and *intent* behind a user's query. Instead of just matching words, it understands concepts. So, if someone searches for "warm running shoes for winter," NeuralSearch can understand that you're looking for something with insulation and good traction, even if those exact words aren't in the product description. It leverages advanced AI, including natural language processing, to grasp context and deliver incredibly relevant results, boosting conversions and cutting down on wasted user time [1, 4, 25].

## Key Advantages of NeuralSearch

* **Understanding Intent:** NeuralSearch deciphers the underlying meaning of a query, not just the literal words. This means fewer "no results found" pages and a much smoother user journey [1, 4].
* **Handling the Long Tail:** Traditional keyword search struggles with the vast majority of queries that aren't exact matches. NeuralSearch excels here, uncovering hidden revenue opportunities within those less common but equally important searches [4, 12].
* **End-to-End AI:** Unlike solutions that just bolt AI onto existing systems, NeuralSearch is designed from the ground up with AI at every stage – from understanding the query, to retrieving results, to ranking them [4, 8].
* **Semantic Understanding:** It uses techniques like vector embeddings to represent words and phrases mathematically, allowing it to grasp semantic relationships and deliver results that are truly conceptually relevant [6, 17, 21].

Essentially, keyword search is like a dictionary – precise but limited. NeuralSearch is like a knowledgeable assistant who understands what you *mean*, even if you don't say it perfectly. This leads to a significantly better user experience and, crucially, better business outcomes.

question: How do I implement faceted search and filtering with InstantSearch?
answer:
You know that feeling when you're trying to find a specific book in a massive library, and you can't remember the exact title, but you know it's a sci-fi novel published in the last five years? That's faceted search in action, and we make that library search lightning fast.

## Streamlining Discovery with Faceted Search

Faceted search is all about helping your users narrow down vast catalogs to exactly what they need, without the frustration. Think of it like this: instead of sifting through thousands of products, users can instantly filter by categories, brands, price ranges, and any other attribute that matters to them. This isn't just about making search pretty; it's about **boosting conversion rates** by making it effortless for customers to find what they're looking for. Companies like Amazon leverage this extensively to guide users through their massive inventories, ensuring relevant results based on the user's intent. [Amazon uses dynamic faceting](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/implementing-faceted-search-with-dynamic-faceting-with-code/) to show different filters for "music" versus "movies," a smart way to enhance the user experience.

With Algolia's InstantSearch, implementing this kind of powerful filtering is designed to be intuitive and highly configurable. You can easily set up **searchable facets** that allow users to even search *within* your filter options, a feature that significantly cuts down on noise. For instance, if you have a long list of brands, a user can type to find the specific brand they want, rather than scrolling endlessly. This capability was a game-changer, reducing the need for complex data preprocessing for each facet. [Search for facet values](https://www.algolia.com/blog/product/search-for-facet-values/) is a prime example of how we empower users to find exactly what they need, faster.

## Building a Seamless Experience

InstantSearch provides pre-built widgets that handle the heavy lifting of displaying facets and applying filters. Whether you're building for web with InstantSearch.js or mobile with our iOS and Android libraries, you get a reactive experience where filters instantly update search results. For mobile, in particular, we offer patterns to manage filter displays effectively, preventing cluttered screens and providing immediate feedback to users, as seen in our [iOS filtering examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/ui-and-ux-patterns/filtering-patterns/ios).

Our goal is to make it incredibly simple to integrate advanced search functionalities that drive real business value.

question: Advisors search for investment products but results ignore client risk profile and portfolio context — how does Algolia's ranking incorporate business signals beyond text relevance?
answer:
You know that feeling when you're trying to find the perfect investment, but all you get is a mountain of irrelevant options? It's like trying to find a needle in a haystack, but the haystack is also on fire and full of hay. Algolia's here to douse the flames and point you directly to that needle.

## Beyond Text: Powering Smarter Search with Business Signals

It's absolutely critical that search results don't just match keywords, they need to reflect real business objectives and user context. For advisors searching for investment products, ignoring a client's risk profile and portfolio context is a non-starter. Algolia’s ranking doesn't just rely on basic text relevance; it’s built to incorporate **business signals** that drive actual value and deliver precisely what's needed.

Think about it:

* **Personalization is Key:** In the world of finance, personalization isn't just a nice-to-have, it's a necessity. AI-enabled personalization allows for tailoring experiences, and this extends directly to search results. By understanding user behavior and preferences, Algolia can surface products that align with an advisor's client objectives, not just their search terms [1, 9, 10].
* **Driving Conversions:** For businesses, the ultimate goal is often conversion, whether that's a sale, a sign-up, or in this case, a successful investment recommendation. Algolia's capabilities are designed to improve user experience, which directly leads to higher conversion rates. For instance, Gymshark saw a **150% increase in order rate** by leveraging Algolia's Recommend product [3, 4, 5]. While this is an e-commerce example, the principle of surfacing the most relevant items to drive desired actions is universal.
* **Leveraging User Behavior:** We capture real-time user interactions as events [21]. This includes clicks, views, and conversions. By analyzing these events, Algolia can understand what advisors are *actually* looking for and what leads to successful outcomes for their clients. This moves beyond simple keyword matching to understanding **user intent** and predicting their next move [15, 16, 17, 19, 20].
* **Strategic Ranking:** Algolia's `search-insights` library allows for tracking events like clicks and conversions, which can be used to influence search ranking [21]. This means that products that are historically more successful for similar advisor queries or client profiles can be ranked higher. We also have the ability to configure [strategy](https://www.algolia.com/doc/libraries/sdk/v1/methods/add-strategy) settings that associate scores with events, directly impacting how results are presented based on business value.

By integrating these business signals, Algolia ensures that search results are not only accurate but also strategically aligned with the advisor's goals and the client's specific needs, moving beyond mere text relevance to deliver actionable insights and drive better outcomes.

question: My searches return no results after I indexed my data — what are the likely causes?
answer:
You know that feeling when you've meticulously organized your entire closet, only to realize you forgot to actually put the clothes *on* the hangers? That's kind of like indexing data but getting no search results. You've done the work, but something's missing in translation.

## No Results? Let's Find the Glitch in the Matrix.

It's frustrating when you've put in the effort to index your data and the search bar returns a big fat nothing. Don't sweat it, this is a common hiccup, and usually, it's a quick fix. The most likely culprits boil down to a few key areas:

* **Data Visibility:** Are the records you're expecting actually making it into the search index? Sometimes, products might be hidden, deleted, or out of stock and configured to be hidden from search results. Algolia won't show what it doesn't "see" as available. For example, if your web shop hides out-of-stock items, and Algolia is configured to mirror that, those items won't appear in search. [14]

* **Configuration Shenanigans:**
    * **Searchable Attributes:** Did you tell Algolia *what* to search for? If your `searchableAttributes` aren't correctly configured, Algolia might not know which fields in your data are important for matching queries. For instance, if you only set `description` as searchable but users are searching for `brand` or `name`, you'll get zero results. [1]
    * **Relevance Settings:** While this usually affects *ranking* rather than *no results*, a severely misconfigured relevance can push all potential matches so far down the list they effectively disappear.

* **Indexing Queue Issues:** If you're using a system like Algolia for Magento, the indexing process often runs through a queue. If this queue crashes or isn't running, your data might not be making it to Algolia, or it could be outdated. [14]

* **Data Integrity:** Sometimes, the issue is with the source data itself. For example, if the "Product Price" index in Magento isn't up to date, products might not sync correctly with Algolia, leading them to be treated as "deleted" and removed from search. [14]

We focus on making sure your search drives revenue, and that starts with ensuring your users *find* what they're looking for.

sources.chunk_text:
# 2 steps to make disorganized content discoverable | Algolia

Today’s users have high standards for their online experiences regardless of the interface or device. A disorganized site, where it is di

# Case study for an online clothing company

This case study helps you understand how to configure Algolia using the dashboard: it’s equally valuable for business users with no technical background and developers. The main goal is to help you **follow the proper steps and get consistently great search results.** The real-world case study gives a better picture of the configuration process.

## Selecting searchable attributes

Attributes are the key-value pairs that compose records, and the records compose an index. You need to select the most relevant attributes a user can search for. For example, on an ecommerce search, [searchable attributes](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/searchable-attributes) could be `name` or `description`.

### Initial list of searchable attributes

*   `name`
*   `description`
*   `price`
*   `image_url`

From the initial list, you wouldn’t select `price` or `image_url`. These attributes are helpful for sorting and display. A user wouldn’t search for those.

### Selecting your searchable attributes

*   `name`
*   `description`

Add `color` and `brand` as searchable attributes. These attributes are often used for filtering and faceting, but people also use them in search queries.

### Adding more attributes

*   `description`
*   `name`
*   `color`
*   `brand`

To further improve relevance, you need to move the most relevant attributes to the top of the list. In this case, users tend to search for a color or a brand as well as an item’s name or description. It then makes more sense to have these attributes higher in the list. Users tend to search with descriptive terms instead of exact item names. To account for it, put `description` first on the list before `name`.

### Listing the most relevant attributes first

*   `description`
*   `name`
*   `brand`, `color`

You could find that `brand` and `color` are just as relevant to your users. You would then put them both on the same line, separated by a comma.

### Listing two equal ranking attributes

*   `description`
*   `name`
*   `brand`, `color`

Long attributes like `description` can be too “noisy” and generate false positives in relevance. In such cases, you can create a derived, shorter attribute and pick just the search-relevant terms. For example, create a `short_description` attribute by selecting the applicable search terms from `description`. To create this shorter attribute, your engineering team needs to configure your data so that it’s available in Algolia’s dashboard.

### Replace a “noisy” attribute with a more efficient one

*   `short_description`
*   `name`
*   `brand`, ‘color\`

You might want to ensure that all the words in `short_description` have the same importance. This means that words in the description’s beginning, middle, and end are uniformly relevant. You can do this with the [`unordered`](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/searchable-attributes/how-to/configuring-searchable-attributes-the-right-way#word-position) modifier. You can do the same for any multi-word attribute, like `item_name`.

### Final list of searchable attributes

*   `unordered(short_description)`
*   `unordered(name)`
*   `brand`, `color`

## Setting custom ranking and sorting

You start with Algolia’s out-of-the-box ranking formula.

### Default ranking

*   Default ranking formula

**You shouldn’t change or remove this ranking formula.** It works out of the box for 99% of use cases.

### Setting custom ranking

You can now [customize your ranking](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/custom-ranking#custom-ranking) by adding some business metrics. It’s typical to add popularity attributes, such as the number of likes or best sellers. It’s also worth using [Click, conversion, and revenue analytics](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/search-analytics/overview) to rank the products with the most successful conversion rate.

### Custom ranking (main index)

*   Default ranking formula
*   `number_of_sells`
*   `popularity`
*   `conversion_rate`

### Sorting by a specific attribute

Consider allowing your users to [sort by](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/sorting) a specific attribute. To do so, you can use Algolia’s sorting capability, which requires you to create a [replica](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/sorting/in-depth/exhaustive-sort#backend-implementation) index for each sort. For example, you can sort by price, from highest-priced items to lowest. To do this, you can create a new replica index called `products_sorted_by_price_descending`.

### Sort by price, highest to lowest (replica one)

*   `price` (sort-by, descending)
*   Default ranking formula

To reverse the order and sort by ascending price, you need to add another replica index, `products_sorted_by_price_ascending`.

### Sort by price, lowest to highest (replica two)

*   `price` (sort-by, ascending)
*   Default ranking formula

Do the same for the date with a third replica and sort from newest to oldest.

### Sort by date, newest to oldest (replica three) –> ### Sort by date, newest to oldest (replica three)

*   `date` (sort-by, descending)
*   Default ranking formula

You now have four indices (a main index and three replicas):

*   Your main index with custom ranking (by best-sellers, popularity, and conversion rate)
*   Your three replicas are:
    *   Sorted by price, descending
    *   Sorted by price, ascending
    *   Sorted by date, descending

For example, for an ascending price sort, the query “red skirt” wouldn’t return items containing “red shirt” because they’re less relevant. Relevant sorting removes noise for users.

### Creating buckets to combine sorts with custom ranking

Consider adding a field like `featured`, a `true` or `false` value that forces all featured items to show up first.

### Show featured items first

*   `featured` (sort-by, descending)
*   Default ranking formula
*   `number_of_sells`
*   `popularity`
*   `conversion_rate`

By doing this, you’re creating two [buckets of results](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/custom-ranking/how-to/boost-or-penalize-some-records), where each bucket is individually ranked. If you have 100 results, 50 of which are `featured`, the first bucket contains all featured items. The featured items rank by textual relevance and custom ranking. The second bucket—the 50 non-featured items—rank by textual relevance and custom ranking. One consequence with buckets is that you may have the most textually relevant record appear in the 51. position because it’s not in the first bucket—that is, it’s not a featured item.

## Promoting content with good user engagement

Consider prioritizing content that has more likes and comments. Depending on how much prioritization you want the content to have, there are two ways to approach this: Rules and custom ranking.

### Rules

Use [Rules](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/rules/rules-overview) to move items with more likes or comments to the top of your results. You can enhance this further by using [optional filters with filter scoring](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/rules/merchandising-and-promoting/in-depth/optional-filters#filter-scoring).

### Custom ranking

Use [custom ranking](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/custom-ranking) for likes, number of comments, or both. For example, assuming you had these attributes in your records, use the likes/comments post date (`postDate`) and the number of comments or likes (`engagement`) as sortable attributes. To do this on the dashboard, go to **Index > Ranking and Sorting** and click the **Add sort-by attribute** button (set the attribute to **Descending** sort order). It’s essential to consider `postDate` since, otherwise, all old comments have equal relevance to newer ones. For example, consider these four items:

1.  One from last year with enormous engagement (100K likes and comments),
2.  One from yesterday with great engagement (50K likes and comments)
3.  One from today with good engagement (10K likes and comments)
4.  One from today with decent engagement (5K likes and comments).

If `postDate` appears before `engagement` in your custom ranking order, post 3 and then post 4 will appear at the top of these results. However, post 2 is relatively recent and has much higher engagement, so it should sit before 3 and 4. The problem is that a single day is too precise for this case.

#### Reducing precision

You can [reduce precision by creating buckets](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/custom-ranking/how-to/controlling-custom-ranking-metrics-precision). For instance, instead of ranking by `postDate`, you could assign a “recency score”:

*   0 if it’s a week old or less
*   1 if it’s between one and two weeks old
*   Up to a score where recency no longer matters (it may not matter if a post is over two years old).

You could also reduce precision for engagement: instead of ranking on a specific number of likes or comments, you could round to the nearest dozen, hundred, or thousand. In such a case, your record would look something like this.

```
[
  {
    "title": "Post #1",
    "postDate": 1569226869, // last year
    "likes_and_comments": 109023, // exact computation
    "recency": 5, // a recency score based on an arbitrary scale of your choosing
    "engagement": 109000 // rounded engagement
  },
  {
    "title": "Post #2",
    "postDate": 1601281269, // yesterday
    "likes_and_comments": 57986,
    "recency": 0, // a week old or less
    "engagement": 58000
  },
  {
    "title": "Post #3",
    "postDate": 1601367669, // today
    "likes_and_comments": 11356,
    "recency": 0,
    "engagement": 11000
  },
  {
    "title": "Post #4",
    "postDate": 1601367669, // today
    "likes_and_comments": 5439,
    "recency": 0,
    "engagement": 5000
  }
]
```

Your custom ranking would be:

1.  `recency`
2.  `engagement`
3.  `postDate`
4.  `likes_and_comments`

## Self-optimizing content

You might want to ensure that items with the most clicks get boosted toward the top of search results. To do this:

1.  Create a `numberOfClicks` attribute in your data
2.  [Collect click events](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/sending-events/concepts/event-types) from your users
3.  Update the `numberOfClicks` values in your records with the collected data
4.  Use [custom ranking](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/custom-ranking) on `numberOfClicks` to boost items with a higher number of clicks (after relevance has been taken into account).

Something to watch out for, though, is that this is self-reinforcing change. The higher these items are in the results, the more likely they are to be clicked, meaning that they will gradually rank higher and higher. You may want to use Algolia’s [A/B testing](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/ab-testing/what-is-ab-testing) feature to check the impact of a change like this.

## Using unique `objectID` s to preserve your data

Some of your records may have duplicate `objectID`s. This causes problems when updating your data.

### Duplicate `objectID`s

*   `objectID`\=12345 (red t-shirt)
*   `objectID`\=12345 (Nike shoes)
*   `objectID`\=67890 (Levi jeans, slim)
*   `objectID`\=67890 (Levi jeans, slimmed)

Here, it looks like two objects share the same `objectID` of `12345`. If you try to index them both, the index retains just one. Make sure that each item has a unique `objectID`. The items with `objectID:67890` look like duplicate records. You should remove one of them.

### Fixed, no duplicate objectID s

*   `objectID`\=12345 (red t-shirt)
*   `objectID`\=23456 (Nike shoes)
*   `objectID`\=67890 (Levi jeans, slim)

## Language settings

If you haven’t set up your [language](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/optimize-search-results/handling-natural-languages-nlp/in-depth/language-specific-configurations#setting-the-search-language) to that of your users, you should do so. Also, make sure that you’ve set [`ignorePlurals`](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/optimize-search-results/handling-natural-languages-nlp/in-depth/language-specific-configurations#ignoring-plurals-and-other-alternative-forms) and [`removeStopWords`](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/optimize-search-results/handling-natural-languages-nlp/in-depth/language-specific-configurations#removing-stop-words) to `true`.

## Defining synonyms

If you’re selling coats, you may notice that some people search for “coats” and others for “jackets”. In your store, these are the same.

### Your synonyms

*   coat=jacket

You may also want to add a [synonym](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/optimize-search-results/adding-synonyms) for shoes and boots so that users looking for shoes can find boots as well. To do this, create a synonym for “shoes” and “boots.”

### Your synonyms

*   coat=jacket
*   shoes=boots

Keep this list up to date with as many synonyms as necessary, but not too many. A long list of synonyms can become unmanageable and create false positives.

## Frontend UI concerns

### Highlighting

Using [highlighting](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/widgets/customize-an-existing-widget/js#highlight-and-snippet-your-search-results) lets your users instantly see why a record is present in the results.

### Without highlighting

Query: **nike** Results

*   Nike Air is the best
*   Magic Nike is built to last

### With highlighting

Query: **nike** Results

*   **Nike** Air is the best
*   Magic **Nike** is built to last

### Instant search results and as-you-type search experience

Implement [instant search results](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/getting-started/how-algolia-works#an-end-to-end-search-solution) with the [InstantSearch libraries](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/what-is-instantsearch/js).

### Setting up facets

Do you have helpful categories in your index, like colors or brands? Add them as [facets](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting) and display them on your UI to let your users filter their results.

## Staying up to date

Ensure that you’re using the latest version of Algolia’s libraries:

*   Frontend library
*   Search client
*   Indexing client

# Inside the Algolia Engine: Index w/ Instant Search

Search engine usage has evolved a lot in the last few years. Today’s users expect instant feedback without having to click on the “enter” button and wait for the page to load to get their results. They also expect the engine to tolerate typing mistakes on the fly without having to validate proposals of query re-phrasing, like the (in)famous “did you mean?”

Of course all these changes have pretty big consequences on the engine itself. In this post, we will concentrate on the way we handle indexing at Algolia, why we do it differently in order to maximize indexing speed and search performance and how we natively address these evolutions in search.

## Introduction to indexing

The goal of the indexing process is to store your data in a specific data-structure that is optimized for search. Without indexing, we would need to scan all the documents in a database to detect which ones match a query. This would take forever and be highly inefficient, ultimately resulting in a terrible user experience. This is one of the reasons we continue to focus on optimizing indexing speed as part of our overall efforts to make search more efficient.

The main part of the indexing process is to create a data-structure that contains a mapping of each word to the associated list of documents containing this word. One mapping of the word to the list of documents is called an inverted list, with the name coming  from the inversion of the space: instead of having documents that contains words, we have now words with the list of documents containing each word. The concept is similar to the index you have at the end of a book, except all words are in the index.

There are a lot of algorithms to store those inverted lists in an efficient way while keeping good performance. Most of those techniques are pretty old and well described in the very good [Managing Gigabytes](https://books.google.fr/books/about/Managing_Gigabytes.html?id=2F74jyPl48EC&redir_esc=y) book.

The creation of those inverted lists can be decomposed in two big parts:

*   For each document, we extract the list of words and build a hash-table that associates words to documents
*   When all documents are processed, we compute an on-disk binary data-structure containing the mapping of words to documents. This data-structure is the index we will use to process queries.

## How search-as-you-type is usually implemented

As-you-type display of search results was introduced by Google in 2010 as “Google Instant” and later referred to as “Instant Search.” Users love this experience as it creates a seamless interaction with the data they are looking for! It removes the intermediate steps such as query validation, “did-you-mean” re-phrasing and decreases the time spent waiting for the results to show up! It also means indexing and search have to be redesigned because you don’t simply search for words anymore, but also for partially written words (prefixes) and it needs to be very fast!  
There are four main different approaches engineers use today to build this type of instant search:

### **1) Autocomplete (also called Suggest)**

This approach is the most commonly used and is based on a static data-structure on the side of the search engine. You can see it as a big dictionary in the form of a [Trie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trie) (special type of Tree that associates string to an output, all the descendants of a node have a common prefix of the string).

**Advantages:**

*   Very fast response time (usually few milliseconds)
*   Scales well, can easily handle thousands of queries per second on one machine

**Drawbacks:**

*   This is not a search engine, you have to search for a sub-string on the pre-defined list of expressions in the dictionary. In terms of user-experience, it often leads to very frustrating “no results” or “bad results” because of this limitation
*   The relevance is not the same as the search engine itself, it is just a static dictionary. It works well if you propose “frequent queries,” but starts to have weird effects if you use it to search on the same records as the fully-featured search engine itself. The relevance will be different, of lesser quality and will lead to a lot of confusion.

### **2) Index prefixes**

This approach is simply to build an inverted list for all prefixes of a word instead of just the word itself. For example instead of just having an inverted list for the word “engine”, you will also have one for “e”, “en”, “eng”, “engi” and “engin”. Those lists will usually contain a specific indicator that allows at query time to make the difference between a prefix and an exact word (both exact and prefix inverted lists will be queried with a preference for exact).

**Advantages:**

*   Fast resolution of prefixes as they are pre-computed
*   Ability to keep the relevance of the search-engine for all interaction with the engine, including as-you-type experience (user is never lost between two different ways to rank results)

**Drawbacks:**

*   Increase the time to publish results because the indexing process is far more expensive than without inverted list and consumes far more memory (inverted list are kept in memory)
*   Increase a lot the size of the index, which reduces a lot the likelihood of fitting in memory and so can hurt performance (even on a SSD)

### **3) ngrams computation**

This approach is very similar to the indexing of all prefixes but it limits the computation of prefixes by allowing a minimum and a maximum number of letters in the prefix. The main advantage is to reduce the cost compared to the indexing of all prefixes, if you query a prefix that is bigger than the maximum number of letters, the query will be automatically be transformed into a query on several ngrams (queries with fewer letters than the minimum will not trigger the prefix search).

Advantages and drawbacks are the same as the indexing of all prefixes; it is just a small improvement to reduce a bit the cost of indexing by adding more computation in the search.

### **4) Search prefixes at search time**

This approach keeps the indexing process pretty light by keeping only one inverted list per word but it requires to do all the processing at query-time. For example if you perform the query ‘a’, it will mean looking for all words that start by ‘a’ in a big dictionary and then perform a query with all those words in a big OR operand. This approach is very expensive and usually people that select this approach start the prefix search after a certain number of letters in the prefix to avoid having queries that are too expensive.

**Advantages:**

*   No indexing overhead for prefix searches
*   No compromises on the relevance of the search-engine

**Drawbacks:**

*   Produces very complex and slow queries
*   Only works if the number of documents and words is very small, it does not scale

Engineers working on search have no other choice than spending time to test those different approaches and select the one that seems the best for their use cases, unfortunately all of them have serious drawbacks.

## A different approach

At the beginning of Algolia, we worked on a different product: an offline search engine SDK for mobile app developers. And we wanted this engine to provide a fast instant search experience on old devices like an iPhone 3G.

None of the standard approaches described above would have worked on such a low-end hardware, so we had to design a new way to do the indexing with very little RAM and CPU. All that while maintaining an instant search experience (one of the purpose of indexing being to speed up the search operations).

Having already worked on multiple data-structures for search engines prior to Algolia helped me a lot in designing a different way to solve the problem. The first inspiration came from a generational string B-tree I built several years ago. It was pretty similar to what the [LevelDB](https://github.com/google/leveldb) fast key-value store developed by Google is doing (associate an arbitrary size value to an arbitrary size string).

First, I knew that for Algolia I wanted to have a generational data-structure.

### **Generational data-structure**

Having a generational data-structure makes updating the data a lot faster. Typically, you’ll create two (or more) “generations” of your data-structure. A big one containing the “old” data, and a small one containing the “new” data.

Then, if you need to add/update/delete an object, you’ll update a smaller structure containing only the “new” information, which is much faster.

### **Merge of Generational data-structure**

At some point (generally when your smallest generation reaches a certain size), you’ll need to merge several generations into one for a better efficiency. This operation is called a merge.

In order to have an efficient merge, each generation needs to be sorted, so that you can scan them in parallel while generating the result (merge of the parallel scan is done via a standard heap-merge algorithm).

### **Radix tree**

For Algolia, I decided to represent the words that will be searchable as a [radix tree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_tree) (space-optimized representation of a Trie, below we look at what a radix tree is in practice).

First because a radix tree is sorted, which allowed us to have an efficient merge and to use multiple generations of the tree.

Second because it works very well to process the typo-tolerance in a very efficient way (more details on the typo-tolerance will come in another post).

It is easy to build a radix tree in memory before writing it on disk, but it consumes a lot of RAM. And I wanted to use the least amount of RAM possible, because:

1.  For the initial offline search SDK, we simply didn’t have enough RAM on an iPhone 3G.
2.  For our current SaaS engine, we want the RAM to be focused on making search fast (the indices are all stored on RAM for search), which means indexing should consume the minimum amount of RAM.

What’s great is that you can actually write a radix tree on the fly without having to build the tree in memory if you’re indexing a list of sorted words.

For example, let’s consider that the dataset we want to search on contains 5 objects, each having a single word. Each object will be represented by a value from 1 to 5.

Normally, multiple objects could have the same word, so the leaf would have a list of values. But for simplicity’s sake, we’ll take records containing different words.

![dictionary1-e1462180935434.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary1-e1462180935434.png)

Dictionary example mapping a word to an integer

Each node of our radix tree will contain a string.

If the node is a leaf, it has a value associated to the corresponding object having this word.

If the node is not a leaf, it can have an associated value, but it can also just be an internal node representing a shared prefix.

The radix-tree we’re going to build will look like this:

![dictionary-2-e1462181068823.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-2-e1462181068823.png)

Radix-tree of the previous dictionary

Now, let’s build our radix-tree. Seems simple now enough, right? The naive approach is to build the complete data-structure in memory and then to dump it on disk. We do not want to use this approach because we want to keep as much memory as possible on our servers to hosts the indices of our users and provide a fast search! This is why we designed all the indexing to use as little memory as possible while optimizing speed of indexing.

Here’s the trick: we can build this data-structure on-disk directly from the list of words and with only a few kilobytes of memory. To do that, we’ll flush from the RAM the nodes as soon as possible, by building the tree on the fly.

The first step is to order alphanumerically the words of the documents we want to index (which is already done in our example).

Then, we’re going to build our tree using a [depth-first search](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depth-first_search): since a radix tree is sorted in the alphanumeric order, it’ll be easy to flush nodes to the disk as soon as we don’t need them.

1) Add the word Fast

![dictionary-3-e1462181232517.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-3-e1462181232517.png)

2) Add the word Faster

![dictionary-4-e1462181279567.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-4-e1462181279567.png)

3) Add the word Test

![dictionary4Fixed.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary4Fixed.png)

4) Flush the branch “fast” to disk

![dictionary5Fixed.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary5Fixed.png)

5) Add the word toaster

![dicationary-7-e1462181493671.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-7-e1462181493671.png)

6) Flush the branch “est”

![dicationary-8-e1462181678105.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-8-e1462181678105.png)

7) Add the word toasting

![dicationary-9-e1462181731807.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-9-e1462181731807.png)

8) Finally, flush the branch “er”, then “ing”, “oast” and finally “t”

This process is a very efficient way to build a tree because it only keeps in memory a very small number of nodes (in the worst case scenario you will have a number of nodes in memory that is equal to the longest key in the tree, in our case we always have fewer than 100 nodes in memory).Now that this is done, let’s focus on the next part: prefix search!

### **Prefix search**

Remember that we wanted an instant search experience. This means that the engine must be able to retrieve results as-you-type, at each character.

If you type “f”, you want to retrieve the results “fast” and “faster”, so the engine must know that both these words contain the prefix “f”.

And we don’t want to calculate the association prefix-object for all the prefixes, which would take a lot of time and memory, but only when it’s really necessary.

The good news is that our radix tree can tell us precisely where we need to store prefixes: all the nodes in the tree which have children.

In our example, it is only necessary for the nodes “fast”, “t” and “toast”.

![dicationary-10-e1462181862124.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-10-e1462181862124.png)

Radix-tree with prefix inverted list computed

You see what I just did right there? Yes! the prefixes can be computed on the fly too! We just need to remember what was in the last few branches that we flushed.

For example, if I just flushed the branch “est”, I’ll remember that the prefix “t” is associated to the object 3 (test). I’ll do the same with “toaster” (object 4) and “toasting” (object 5). And when we’re done processing all the words beginning with “t”, I’ll know that this node should contain the objects 3, 4 and 5.

## A few numbers

This way to build prefixes has been built into our engine since 2012 and we index billions of jobs every day with this algorithm. We have measured the impact on a lot of different indices, the impact on search is pretty obvious as we always have the prefix inverted list when we do a query (and we’ll have a lot of RAM handy), but the impact on indexing time and on the size are less obvious. Here is a small summary of the impacts that are very small compared to the advantages it gives at query time:

|     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | Minimum | Average | Maximum |
| Impact of prefixes on index size | +9% | +21% | +43% |
| Impact of prefixes on indexing speed | +3% | +12% | +17% |

This approach is significantly better than any of the other approaches presented before, it is actually optimal at query time and has a very reasonable impact on indexing performance!

This optimization of the prefixed indexing is just one of the numerous optimizations we have built in the Algolia engine. The reason we built it is because of our focus on performance and the fact that our engine was developed specifically to allow for the strong constraints of mobiles devices. We also had to think a bit out of the box, mainly by considering the word dictionary and inverted lists as one unique data-structure instead of two independent one.

I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this post and continuing to explain how our engine is implemented internally, transparency is part of our DNA! 🙂

We recommend to read the other posts of this series:

*   [Part 1 – indexing vs. search](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-1-indexing-vs-search/)
*   [Part 3 – query processing](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-3-query-processing/)
*   [Part 4 – Textual Relevance](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-enginepart-4-textual-relevance/)
*   [Part 5 – Highlighting, a Cornerstone of Search UX](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-5-highlighting-a-cornerstone-to-search-ux/)
*   [Part 6 – Handling Synonyms the Right Way](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-engine-part-6-handling-synonyms-the-right-way/)
*   [Part 7 – Better relevance via dedup at query time](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-engine-part-7-better-relevance-via-dedup-at-query-time/)
*   [Part 8 – Handling Advanced Search Use Cases](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-engine-part-8-handling-advanced-search-use-cases/)

Second because it works very well to process the typo-tolerance in a very efficient way (more details on the typo-tolerance will come in another post).

It is easy to build a radix tree in memory before writing it on disk, but it consumes a lot of RAM. And I wanted to use the least amount of RAM possible, because:

1.  For the initial offline search SDK, we simply didn’t have enough RAM on an iPhone 3G.
2.  For our current SaaS engine, we want the RAM to be focused on making search fast (the indices are all stored on RAM for search), which means indexing should consume the minimum amount of RAM.

What’s great is that you can actually write a radix tree on the fly without having to build the tree in memory if you’re indexing a list of sorted words.

For example, let’s consider that the dataset we want to search on contains 5 objects, each having a single word. Each object will be represented by a value from 1 to 5.

Normally, multiple objects could have the same word, so the leaf would have a list of values. But for simplicity’s sake, we’ll take records containing different words.

![dictionary1-e1462180935434.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary1-e1462180935434.png)

Dictionary example mapping a word to an integer

Each node of our radix tree will contain a string.

If the node is a leaf, it has a value associated to the corresponding object having this word.

If the node is not a leaf, it can have an associated value, but it can also just be an internal node representing a shared prefix.

The radix-tree we’re going to build will look like this:

![dictionary-2-e1462181068823.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-2-e1462181068823.png)

Radix-tree of the previous dictionary

Now, let’s build our radix-tree. Seems simple now enough, right? The naive approach is to build the complete data-structure in memory and then to dump it on disk. We do not want to use this approach because we want to keep as much memory as possible on our servers to hosts the indices of our users and provide a fast search! This is why we designed all the indexing to use as little memory as possible while optimizing speed of indexing.

Here’s the trick: we can build this data-structure on-disk directly from the list of words and with only a few kilobytes of memory. To do that, we’ll flush from the RAM the nodes as soon as possible, by building the tree on the fly.

The first step is to order alphanumerically the words of the documents we want to index (which is already done in our example).

Then, we’re going to build our tree using a [depth-first search](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depth-first_search): since a radix tree is sorted in the alphanumeric order, it’ll be easy to flush nodes to the disk as soon as we don’t need them.

1) Add the word Fast

![dictionary-3-e1462181232517.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-3-e1462181232517.png)

2) Add the word Faster

![dictionary-4-e1462181279567.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-4-e1462181279567.png)

3) Add the word Test

![dictionary4Fixed.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary4Fixed.png)

4) Flush the branch “fast” to disk

![dictionary5Fixed.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary5Fixed.png)

5) Add the word toaster

![dicationary-7-e1462181493671.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-7-e1462181493671.png)

6) Flush the branch “est”

![dicationary-8-e1462181678105.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-8-e1462181678105.png)

7) Add the word toasting

![dicationary-9-e1462181731807.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-9-e1462181731807.png)

8) Finally, flush the branch “er”, then “ing”, “oast” and finally “t”

This process is a very efficient way to build a tree because it only keeps in memory a very small number of nodes (in the worst case scenario you will have a number of nodes in memory that is equal to the longest key in the tree, in our case we always have fewer than 100 nodes in memory).Now that this is done, let’s focus on the next part: prefix search!

### **Prefix search**

Remember that we wanted an instant search experience. This means that the engine must be able to retrieve results as-you-type, at each character.

If you type “f”, you want to retrieve the results “fast” and “faster”, so the engine must know that both these words contain the prefix “f”.

And we don’t want to calculate the association prefix-object for all the prefixes, which would take a lot of time and memory, but only when it’s really necessary.

The good news is that our radix tree can tell us precisely where we need to store prefixes: all the nodes in the tree which have children.

In our example, it is only necessary for the nodes “fast”, “t” and “toast”.

![dicationary-10-e1462181862124.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-10-e1462181862124.png)

Radix-tree with prefix inverted list computed

You see what I just did right there? Yes! the prefixes can be computed on the fly too! We just need to remember what was in the last few branches that we flushed.

For example, if I just flushed the branch “est”, I’ll remember that the prefix “t” is associated to the object 3 (test). I’ll do the same with “toaster” (object 4) and “toasting” (object 5). And when we’re done processing all the words beginning with “t”, I’ll know that this node should contain the objects 3, 4 and 5.

## A few numbers

This way to build prefixes has been built into our engine since 2012 and we index billions of jobs every day with this algorithm. We have measured the impact on a lot of different indices, the impact on search is pretty obvious as we always have the prefix inverted list when we do a query (and we’ll have a lot of RAM handy), but the impact on indexing time and on the size are less obvious. Here is a small summary of the impacts that are very small compared to the advantages it gives at query time:

|     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | Minimum | Average | Maximum |
| Impact of prefixes on index size | +9% | +21% | +43% |
| Impact of prefixes on indexing speed | +3% | +12% | +17% |

This approach is significantly better than any of the other approaches presented before, it is actually optimal at query time and has a very reasonable impact on indexing performance!

This optimization of the prefixed indexing is just one of the numerous optimizations we have built in the Algolia engine. The reason we built it is because of our focus on performance and the fact that our engine was developed specifically to allow for the strong constraints of mobiles devices. We also had to think a bit out of the box, mainly by considering the word dictionary and inverted lists as one unique data-structure instead of two independent one.

I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this post and continuing to explain how our engine is implemented internally, transparency is part of our DNA! 🙂

We recommend to read the other posts of this series:

*   [Part 1 – indexing vs. search](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-1-indexing-vs-search/)
*   [Part 3 – query processing](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-3-query-processing/)
*   [Part 4 – Textual Relevance](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-enginepart-4-textual-relevance/)
*   [Part 5 – Highlighting, a Cornerstone of Search UX](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-5-highlighting-a-cornerstone-to-search-ux/)
*   [Part 6 – Handling Synonyms the Right Way](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-engine-part-6-handling-synonyms-the-right-way/)
*   [Part 7 – Better relevance via dedup at query time](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-engine-part-7-better-relevance-via-dedup-at-query-time/)
*   [Part 8 – Handling Advanced Search Use Cases](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-engine-part-8-handling-advanced-search-use-cases/)

sources.doc_summary: The document explains Algolia's innovative indexing method designed to enhance search performance by allowing instant results, accommodating user input flexibly, and minimizing resource usage. It critiques common approaches to instant search, like autocomplete and prefix indexing, which often compromise relevance or efficiency, and instead introduces Algolia’s unique use of a generational data-structure and on-the-fly radix tree construction to optimize both indexing speed and memory consumption. Overall, this methodology enables fast, relevant, and resource-efficient search capabilities, particularly suitable for mobile and constrained environments.

# Inside the Algolia Engine: Index w/ Instant Search

Search engine usage has evolved a lot in the last few years. Today’s users expect instant feedback without having to click on the “enter” button and wait for the page to load to get their results. They also expect the engine to tolerate typing mistakes on the fly without having to validate proposals of query re-phrasing, like the (in)famous “did you mean?”

Of course all these changes have pretty big consequences on the engine itself. In this post, we will concentrate on the way we handle indexing at Algolia, why we do it differently in order to maximize indexing speed and search performance and how we natively address these evolutions in search.

## Introduction to indexing

The goal of the indexing process is to store your data in a specific data-structure that is optimized for search. Without indexing, we would need to scan all the documents in a database to detect which ones match a query. This would take forever and be highly inefficient, ultimately resulting in a terrible user experience. This is one of the reasons we continue to focus on optimizing indexing speed as part of our overall efforts to make search more efficient.

The main part of the indexing process is to create a data-structure that contains a mapping of each word to the associated list of documents containing this word. One mapping of the word to the list of documents is called an inverted list, with the name coming  from the inversion of the space: instead of having documents that contains words, we have now words with the list of documents containing each word. The concept is similar to the index you have at the end of a book, except all words are in the index.

There are a lot of algorithms to store those inverted lists in an efficient way while keeping good performance. Most of those techniques are pretty old and well described in the very good [Managing Gigabytes](https://books.google.fr/books/about/Managing_Gigabytes.html?id=2F74jyPl48EC&redir_esc=y) book.

The creation of those inverted lists can be decomposed in two big parts:

*   For each document, we extract the list of words and build a hash-table that associates words to documents
*   When all documents are processed, we compute an on-disk binary data-structure containing the mapping of words to documents. This data-structure is the index we will use to process queries.

## How search-as-you-type is usually implemented

As-you-type display of search results was introduced by Google in 2010 as “Google Instant” and later referred to as “Instant Search.” Users love this experience as it creates a seamless interaction with the data they are looking for! It removes the intermediate steps such as query validation, “did-you-mean” re-phrasing and decreases the time spent waiting for the results to show up! It also means indexing and search have to be redesigned because you don’t simply search for words anymore, but also for partially written words (prefixes) and it needs to be very fast!  
There are four main different approaches engineers use today to build this type of instant search:

### **1) Autocomplete (also called Suggest)**

This approach is the most commonly used and is based on a static data-structure on the side of the search engine. You can see it as a big dictionary in the form of a [Trie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trie) (special type of Tree that associates string to an output, all the descendants of a node have a common prefix of the string).

**Advantages:**

*   Very fast response time (usually few milliseconds)
*   Scales well, can easily handle thousands of queries per second on one machine

**Drawbacks:**

*   This is not a search engine, you have to search for a sub-string on the pre-defined list of expressions in the dictionary. In terms of user-experience, it often leads to very frustrating “no results” or “bad results” because of this limitation
*   The relevance is not the same as the search engine itself, it is just a static dictionary. It works well if you propose “frequent queries,” but starts to have weird effects if you use it to search on the same records as the fully-featured search engine itself. The relevance will be different, of lesser quality and will lead to a lot of confusion.

### **2) Index prefixes**

This approach is simply to build an inverted list for all prefixes of a word instead of just the word itself. For example instead of just having an inverted list for the word “engine”, you will also have one for “e”, “en”, “eng”, “engi” and “engin”. Those lists will usually contain a specific indicator that allows at query time to make the difference between a prefix and an exact word (both exact and prefix inverted lists will be queried with a preference for exact).

**Advantages:**

*   Fast resolution of prefixes as they are pre-computed
*   Ability to keep the relevance of the search-engine for all interaction with the engine, including as-you-type experience (user is never lost between two different ways to rank results)

**Drawbacks:**

*   Increase the time to publish results because the indexing process is far more expensive than without inverted list and consumes far more memory (inverted list are kept in memory)
*   Increase a lot the size of the index, which reduces a lot the likelihood of fitting in memory and so can hurt performance (even on a SSD)

### **3) ngrams computation**

This approach is very similar to the indexing of all prefixes but it limits the computation of prefixes by allowing a minimum and a maximum number of letters in the prefix. The main advantage is to reduce the cost compared to the indexing of all prefixes, if you query a prefix that is bigger than the maximum number of letters, the query will be automatically be transformed into a query on several ngrams (queries with fewer letters than the minimum will not trigger the prefix search).

Advantages and drawbacks are the same as the indexing of all prefixes; it is just a small improvement to reduce a bit the cost of indexing by adding more computation in the search.

### **4) Search prefixes at search time**

This approach keeps the indexing process pretty light by keeping only one inverted list per word but it requires to do all the processing at query-time. For example if you perform the query ‘a’, it will mean looking for all words that start by ‘a’ in a big dictionary and then perform a query with all those words in a big OR operand. This approach is very expensive and usually people that select this approach start the prefix search after a certain number of letters in the prefix to avoid having queries that are too expensive.

**Advantages:**

*   No indexing overhead for prefix searches
*   No compromises on the relevance of the search-engine

**Drawbacks:**

*   Produces very complex and slow queries
*   Only works if the number of documents and words is very small, it does not scale

Engineers working on search have no other choice than spending time to test those different approaches and select the one that seems the best for their use cases, unfortunately all of them have serious drawbacks.

## A different approach

At the beginning of Algolia, we worked on a different product: an offline search engine SDK for mobile app developers. And we wanted this engine to provide a fast instant search experience on old devices like an iPhone 3G.

None of the standard approaches described above would have worked on such a low-end hardware, so we had to design a new way to do the indexing with very little RAM and CPU. All that while maintaining an instant search experience (one of the purpose of indexing being to speed up the search operations).

Having already worked on multiple data-structures for search engines prior to Algolia helped me a lot in designing a different way to solve the problem. The first inspiration came from a generational string B-tree I built several years ago. It was pretty similar to what the [LevelDB](https://github.com/google/leveldb) fast key-value store developed by Google is doing (associate an arbitrary size value to an arbitrary size string).

First, I knew that for Algolia I wanted to have a generational data-structure.

### **Generational data-structure**

Having a generational data-structure makes updating the data a lot faster. Typically, you’ll create two (or more) “generations” of your data-structure. A big one containing the “old” data, and a small one containing the “new” data.

Then, if you need to add/update/delete an object, you’ll update a smaller structure containing only the “new” information, which is much faster.

### **Merge of Generational data-structure**

At some point (generally when your smallest generation reaches a certain size), you’ll need to merge several generations into one for a better efficiency. This operation is called a merge.

In order to have an efficient merge, each generation needs to be sorted, so that you can scan them in parallel while generating the result (merge of the parallel scan is done via a standard heap-merge algorithm).

### **Radix tree**

For Algolia, I decided to represent the words that will be searchable as a [radix tree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_tree) (space-optimized representation of a Trie, below we look at what a radix tree is in practice).

First because a radix tree is sorted, which allowed us to have an efficient merge and to use multiple generations of the tree.

Second because it works very well to process the typo-tolerance in a very efficient way (more details on the typo-tolerance will come in another post).

It is easy to build a radix tree in memory before writing it on disk, but it consumes a lot of RAM. And I wanted to use the least amount of RAM possible, because:

1.  For the initial offline search SDK, we simply didn’t have enough RAM on an iPhone 3G.
2.  For our current SaaS engine, we want the RAM to be focused on making search fast (the indices are all stored on RAM for search), which means indexing should consume the minimum amount of RAM.

![dictionary1-e1462180935434.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary1-e1462180935434.png)

Radix-tree of the previous dictionary

We do not want to use this approach because we want to keep as much memory as possible on our servers to hosts the indices of our users and provide a fast search! 1) Add the word Fast

![dictionary-3-e1462181232517.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-3-e1462181232517.png)

2) Add the word Faster

![dictionary-4-e1462181279567.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary-4-e1462181279567.png)

3) Add the word Test

![dictionary4Fixed.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary4Fixed.png)

4) Flush the branch “fast” to disk

![dictionary5Fixed.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dictionary5Fixed.png)

5) Add the word toaster

![dicationary-7-e1462181493671.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-7-e1462181493671.png)

6) Flush the branch “est”

![dicationary-8-e1462181678105.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-8-e1462181678105.png)

7) Add the word toasting

![dicationary-9-e1462181731807.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-9-e1462181731807.png)

8) Finally, flush the branch “er”, then “ing”, “oast” and finally “t”

![dicationary-10-e1462181862124.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/dicationary-10-e1462181862124.png)

Radix-tree with prefix inverted list computed

You see what I just did right there? Yes! the prefixes can be computed on the fly too! We just need to remember what was in the last few branches that we flushed.

Here is a small summary of the impacts that are very small compared to the advantages it gives at query time:

|     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | Minimum | Average | Maximum |
| Impact of prefixes on index size | +9% | +21% | +43% |
| Impact of prefixes on indexing speed | +3% | +12% | +17% |

This approach is significantly better than any of the other approaches presented before, it is actually optimal at query time and has a very reasonable impact on indexing performance!

The reason we built it is because of our focus on performance and the fact that our engine was developed specifically to allow for the strong constraints of mobiles devices. *   [Part 1 – indexing vs. search](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-1-indexing-vs-search/)
*   [Part 3 – query processing](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-3-query-processing/)
*   [Part 4 – Textual Relevance](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-enginepart-4-textual-relevance/)
*   [Part 5 – Highlighting, a Cornerstone of Search UX](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-5-highlighting-a-cornerstone-to-search-ux/)

It explains how traditional techniques for search indexing often involve considerable trade-offs in efficiency and relevance, and introduces Algolia's innovative generational data-structure and radix trees that minimize memory usage while maximizing indexing and search capabilities.
