# Building real-time analytics APIs at scale

We recently redesigned our analytics API from the ground up, in order to provide near real-time analytics to our customers on billions of search queries per day. Here’s how we did it.

## Redsigning our Analytics system from the ground up

Our first analytics system started as a small side project. It consisted of batches of compressed log files being sent from all our search servers to a central storage service, before being pushed to an Elasticsearch cluster, from which we served the analytics queries.

Naturally, this system had its limitations. Firstly, we needed to pave the way for our new Click Analytics feature, where we wanted to leverage a more relational model. Doing so with Elasticsearch, where documents are independent, would have been too cumbersome.

Secondly, with dozens of billions of records to handle across many nodes, managing such cluster was becoming a full-time job.

Our search analytics provides our customers with insights about how their search is being used. These insights can easily grow in size. They range from overviews, like the number of searches or the number of unique users, to actionable business insights such as ‘What categories are users most interested in?’ or ‘Which queries return no results?’. All of these can also be done over specific time ranges which, in some instances, can be very very large.

What follows is an explanation of the tech choices we made when rebuilding our analytics from the ground up, as well as an overview of some of the design principles we put to use.

## Choosing the datastore for Analytics

The top level requirements we needed to meet were:

*   Handle ingestion, storage, and deletion of billions of events per day.
*   Return results in sub-second for most queries, regardless of the time range being requested.
*   Scale with us. We’re doubling our scale every year, so the selected solution should be able to keep up with that growth painlessly. Ideally by just adding more machines.

We started by evaluating powerhouses like [RedShift](https://aws.amazon.com/redshift/), [BigQuery](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/) and [ClickHouse](https://clickhouse.yandex/). While they are certainly good options for data warehousing, we did not find them good enough for real-time analytics workflow.

In our case, the focus is on performing sub-second analytics queries rather than long running analysis.

However, achieving sub-second aggregation performances on very large datasets is prohibitively expensive with RedShift, and not possible with BigQuery.

Additionally, another issue we found with BigQuery was that the pricing is driven by usage, not storage. As such, we didn’t consider it a safe choice to power a public API.

For ClickHouse, we were curious to try it out but ultimately found that there would be a lot of extra engineering overhead required to host, maintain and fine tune it.

Finally, our search led us to [Citus Data](https://www.citusdata.com/) and their Citus extension for PostgreSQL, that makes it seamless to scale Postgres by distributing tables and queries across multiple nodes.

Citus Data also provides [several extensions](https://www.citusdata.com/blog/2017/12/27/real-time-analytics-dashboards-with-citus/) that are well suited to real-time analytics such as [HLL](https://www.citusdata.com/blog/2017/04/04/distributed_count_distinct_with_postgresql/) (HyperLogLog) and [TopN](https://www.citusdata.com/blog/2018/03/27/topn-for-your-postgres-database/). The former is an algorithm for fast approximative distinct count, and the latter acts like a heap, which allows keeping top items sorted by frequency in a JSONB field.

If we opted for this solution, we could spread our customers’ data across many nodes, leverage collocation, and precompute metrics. As a bonus, we would still get the benefit of having a relational database running up-to-date Postgres instances.

![schema1.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/schema1.png)

With the data store decision made, here’s how the rest of our analytics solution works in practice.

## Creating near real-time analytics

Achieving sub-second analytical queries doesn’t come out of the box though. We achieve this both by distributing data across shards and by using a roll-up approach.

To achieve this we have to perform the following steps:

*   Ingest raw events into the system.
*   Periodically aggregate events into dedicated rollup tables.
*   Query the roll-up tables and get the result in milliseconds.

### Ingestion

We take advantage of the Postgres [COPY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/10/static/sql-copy.html) command to insert batch events into Citus. As we’ll see below, we never query the raw data directly so those tables can stay very simple schema-wise.

Without a lot of indices to update, the performance of insertion operations is quite impressive. Citus [advertises ingestion of up to 7M rows per second](https://www.citusdata.com/blog/2016/06/15/copy-postgresql-distributed-tables) (that’s 600B rows per day!) and our own benchmarks showed similar trends.

We distribute our data by customer. A single customer’s data lives on the same shard so we can take advantage of collocation. A request targeting a single customer app will only ever need to target a single Postgres instance.

### Rollup

We don’t serve metrics from raw events. At some point, datasets, even for a single customer, can become too large to process on the fly if you’re looking for sub-second response time.

[As a rule of thumb](https://www.citusdata.com/blog/2017/09/29/what-performance-can-you-expect-from-postgres/), you can expect to aggregate 1M rows per second per core with PostgreSQL.

We instead use roll-up tables. Rollup tables hold pre-computed metrics for a given time range.

As we’ve seen in the introduction, we often need to return tops and distinct count. This is made easy thanks to the TOPN and HLL extensions mentioned above.

Here’s a simplified rollup function that covers several use cases:

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
|     | CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION compute\_5min\_rollups(start\_time TIMESTAMP, end\_time TIMESTAMP) |
|     | RETURNS void LANGUAGE PLPGSQL AS $function$ |
|     | BEGIN |
|     | EXECUTE $$ |
|     | INSERT INTO rollups\_5min |
|     | SELECT |
|     | date\_trunc('seconds', (timestamp - TIMESTAMP 'epoch') / 300) \* 300 + TIMESTAMP 'epoch' AS minute, |
|     | app\_id, |
|     | timestamp, |
|     | count(\*) AS query\_count, |
|     | hll\_add\_agg(hll\_hash\_bigint(user\_id)) AS user\_count, |
|     | topn\_add\_agg(query) AS top\_queries |
|     | FROM queries |
|     | WHERE timestamp >= $1 AND timestamp <= $2 |
|     | GROUP BY app\_id, minute; $$ |
|     | USING start\_time, end\_time; |
|     | END; |
|     | $function$ |

[view raw](https://gist.github.com/martyndavies/e61ce1be6a2620ee1e62c5114758b997/raw/f6f217df110f7e99a8a198c19bea905c220456d1/rollup.sql)[rollup.sql](https://gist.github.com/martyndavies/e61ce1be6a2620ee1e62c5114758b997#file-rollup-sql) hosted with ❤ by [GitHub](https://github.com/)

This function aggregates all queries received in a given time range into buckets of 5 minutes, and computes their count, the unique number of users (using HLL), and keeps the top queries, along with their respective count (using TOPN).

This function will be executed concurrently across all nodes.

For our analytics solution we have several levels of rollups. We aggregate events every 5 minutes, and further aggregate them by day. We do this for several reasons:

*   It means the metrics returned by the API are updated frequently. Roughly every 5 minutes, the data is refreshed, so customers don’t have to wait long until their queries are reflected in the dashboard.
*   To compress our dataset even more, we keep daily aggregates. This means that at some point the granularity is reduced to the day level, but this is acceptable for us. We could even consider rolling our dataset by month after a point, which would allow us to keep offering close to unlimited retention, at the cost of coarser granularity. The opposite is also true, adding intermediate hourly rollup could also be an option in the future.
*   Since we’re precomputing tops, we can’t incrementally append queries to them throughout the day. Doing so would yield very incorrect tops. Instead, we build many tops across the day, and we merge them all at once by EOD. We’re still doing a top of tops, but considering their fine granularity and the fact that we merge them all at once, in the end, we only saw a minimal difference compared to a more exhaustive (and much more computation intensive) approach.

![schema2-1.png](https://www.algolia.com/files/live/sites/algolia-assets/files/blogs/2025/schema2-1.png)

A consequence of such an approach is that once the data is rolled up, we can delete it. We don’t need to keep terabytes of raw events in Citus to serve metrics and deleting data is made easy thanks to Citus’ ability to parallelize deletes across nodes.

### Analytics Queries

The API targets the roll-up tables, never the raw tables. If we compare the number of rows from the raw tables and the roll-up tables, we see a compression ratio ranging from 50,000 to 150 on average (this of course varies based on the dimension picked for the aggregation).

This is what makes this approach work so well. Since ultimately our metrics are pre-computed per day, we can easily understand why we get results in milliseconds across virtually any time range: the amount of data to scan at query-time is trivial.

For instance, to get the count of queries for a given customer for the past week, it’s a quick index scan to fetch 7 rows living on the same instance followed by a sum.

To get the top 1000 searches for the past month, we fetch 30 rows, unnest the top items, and further aggregate them into our final top 1000.

Here are a few simplified example queries to demonstrate:

To get the count of queries:

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
|     | SELECT sum(query\_count) |
|     | FROM (( |
|     | SELECT query\_count |
|     | FROM rollups\_5min |
|     | WHERE timestamp >= $1 AND timestamp < $2 AND app\_id = $3 |
|     | ) UNION ALL ( |
|     | SELECT query\_count |
|     | FROM rollups\_1day |
|     | WHERE timestamp >= $1 AND timestamp < $2 AND app\_id = $3 |
|     | )) a... |

[view raw](https://gist.github.com/martyndavies/d4acbb242133e33f832c678ad1850163/raw/86ac8e702ab6d328594aa32e8d8a3db37a18dd95/query-count-example.sql)[query-count-example.sql](https://gist.github.com/martyndavies/d4acbb242133e33f832c678ad1850163#file-query-count-example-sql) hosted with ❤ by [GitHub](https://github.com/)

Since our metrics first live in the 5min rollup table before being merged into the daily roll-up, we query both tables and union their result at query time.

To get a distinct count of users, we take advantage of the HLL type which can compute the intersection of several HLL field.

SELECT hll\_cardinality(sum(user\_count))::bigint FROM ... WHERE ...

And last, but not least, here’s how fetching the top 10 queries would look:

SELECT (topn(topn\_union\_agg(top\_queries), 10)).\* FROM ... WHERE ...

All our analytics queries follow this pattern, where only a minimal aggregation logic is performed at query time.

## The big picture

Let’s take a step back and have a look at the whole system:

![Our whole architecture for analytics processing](https://blog-api.algolia.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/04/schema3-1-720x284.png)

Before actually inserting our queries into Citus, we first aggregate keystrokes into queries. Most of our customers have an InstantSearch implementation. With InstantSearch, results are obtained as you type. This is a great user experience that under the hood generates many API requests for a given search. For example, searching for ‘Algolia’ may result in up to 7 queries (A, Al, Alg… etc). When it comes to analytics, we only want to provide insights on full queries (someone searched for Algolia once), not the intermediate keystrokes.

To ensure this is the case, we have a pipeline that processes our logs to aggregate the sequence of keystrokes into searches, before pushing them straight into Citus.

We rebuilt this pipeline using Go as our language of choice. We’ve always had a microservice approach at Algolia, and over the past year we started building most our services (with the exception of our [search engine](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/inside-the-algolia-engine-part-1-indexing-vs-search/)) in Go and have been very satisfied so far, both in terms of performance and productivity.

To complete the picture, we rely on Kubernetes on GKE for orchestration, and Google Pub/Sub for communication across services.

## Conclusion

Thanks to Citus and a roll-up based approach, our analytics handles billions of searches per day across thousands of customers, a number that is rapidly growing. So far, we’re very pleased by the performance and scalability of the system and are looking forward to building more products on top of it.

Check out how we’ve migrated our [Citus on AWS to Citus on Azure](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/migrating-citus-on-aws-to-citus-on-azure-our-playbook-and-testing-strategies/) without impacting our users or codebase.
sources.doc_title: Building real-time analytics APIs at scale | Algolia
sources.doc_summary: The document discusses a comprehensive redesign of an analytics API to handle billions of search queries daily, focusing on near real-time analytics. Key decisions included selecting Citus Data for its PostgreSQL extension, enabling scalable data distribution and efficient query performance through roll-up tables that pre-compute metrics for quick access. The strategy emphasizes a robust ingestion pipeline using Go, and architecture that leverages Kubernetes and Google Pub/Sub, achieving impressive analytics capabilities while maintaining ease of management as data volume grows.

question: I'm getting a 403 Forbidden when indexing records — how do I fix it?
answer:
You know when you try to get into a VIP club and the bouncer gives you *that* look? Yeah, that's a 403 Forbidden. Algolia's crawler is getting the cold shoulder from your site.

## Decoding the 403 Forbidden During Indexing

A `403 Forbidden` error during indexing means the Algolia Crawler is being blocked from accessing your site's content. It's like a digital bouncer saying "Nope, you're not getting in here!" This usually boils down to security measures on your end that are preventing the crawler from doing its job.

Here are the most common culprits:

* **Security System Blocks:** Your website might have security systems (like Cloudflare, Google Cloud Armor, or even custom firewall rules) that are misinterpreting the Algolia Crawler as a threat. They're doing their job to protect your site, but they're accidentally blocking legitimate access.
* **Robots.txt Restrictions:** The `robots.txt` file on your server tells search engine crawlers which parts of your site they can and cannot access. If it's too restrictive, it might be blocking the Algolia Crawler.
* **Allowlist/Denylist Issues:** Your server might have an allowlist or denylist configured, and the Algolia Crawler's IP address or user agent isn't on the approved list.

## Getting the Crawler Back In

To resolve this, we need to ensure the Algolia Crawler is on your "nice list."

* **Whitelist the Crawler:** The most direct fix is to add the Algolia Crawler's IP address (34.66.202.43) and its specific user agent (`Algolia Crawler/xx.xx.xx`, where `xx.xx.xx` is the version number) to your server's allowlist or security configurations. This tells your security systems, "Hey, this is okay!"
* **Adjust `robots.txt`:** Review your `robots.txt` file. If you find a directive that blocks the `Algolia Crawler` user agent, you'll need to modify it to grant access. For example, you might add `User-agent: Algolia Crawler` to explicitly allow it. You can find more details on handling this in the [troubleshooting documentation](https://www.algolia.com/doc/tools/crawler/troubleshooting/extraction-issues#responses-to-user-agents).
* **Check Custom Security:** If you have custom security rules implemented at the server level (e.g., in Nginx or Apache configuration), double-check those to ensure they aren't inadvertently blocking the crawler.

By addressing these points, you'll effectively remove the "no entry" sign for the Algolia Crawler and get your records indexed smoothly.

question: Where do I start learning to build search with Algolia as a new developer?
answer:
You know that feeling when you're trying to find a specific tool in a messy toolbox? That's what building search can be like without the right guidance. Algolia makes it so you don't have to dig around forever.

## Getting Your Feet Wet with Algolia Search

As a new developer diving into search with Algolia, the best place to start is by understanding the core concepts and how to get a basic implementation up and running. We want to focus on the value you can deliver quickly, not getting lost in the weeds.

For web development, diving into **React InstantSearch** is a fantastic way to build a robust search interface. It provides pre-built components that make it super fast to get a search experience live. You can see how this comes together in building a [store locator](https://www.algolia.com/blog/engineering/building-a-store-locator-in-react-using-algolia-mapbox-and-twilio-part-2) as a practical example.

If you're on the mobile side, particularly iOS, **InstantSearch iOS** is your go-to. It's designed to help you build a full search experience from scratch, including a search box, results display, and filtering, all with a programmatic approach. You can get started by following the [programmatic setup guide](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/getting-started/how-to/programmatically/ios).

For Android development with a modern, declarative approach, **InstantSearch for Compose** is the way to go. It integrates seamlessly with Jetpack Compose to build your search UI. The [declarative UI guide](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/getting-started/how-to/declarative/android) walks you through setting up dependencies and implementing the core search logic.

Even if you're working with Vue, we've got you covered. The [Autocomplete with Vue guide](https://www.algolia.com/doc/ui-libraries/autocomplete/integrations/using-vue) shows you how to integrate Autocomplete, which is excellent for type-ahead search experiences, directly into your Vue application.

The key is to start with a framework that matches your development environment and allows you to see results quickly. This way, you can focus on the business value of providing a great search experience.

question: We run 6 brand websites with inconsistent search and no shared learnings — how does Algolia handle multi-brand search with personalized results across separate catalogs?
answer:
Trying to manage search across six brand websites without shared learnings is like trying to herd cats wearing roller skates – chaotic and bound to end in a tangled mess. Algolia brings order to that chaos.

## Centralized Control, Decentralized Brilliance

Algolia tackles your multi-brand challenge head-on by allowing you to manage search and discovery across distinct catalogs and brand identities, all from a single platform. Think of it as a master control panel for all your digital storefronts. This means you can implement sophisticated **personalization** strategies that cater to individual user preferences, even across different brand experiences.

We understand that each brand might have its own unique catalog, audience, and even specific B2B entitlement policies [1]. Algolia's architecture supports this by enabling you to configure separate indices for each brand or catalog. This ensures that search results are not only relevant to the brand the user is interacting with but also adhere to any specific business rules, like custom pricing or product visibility for different customer groups in B2B scenarios [8].

## Personalization That Learns and Adapts

The magic happens with our **Personalization** feature [2, 4]. It builds user profiles based on their behavior – what they view, click, and purchase. This allows us to tailor search results, pushing products and content that are most likely to resonate with each individual user.

For instance, imagine a user browsing your apparel brand, then later visiting your electronics brand. Algolia can leverage their past interactions to inform their experience on the second site, making it feel more intuitive and relevant. This isn't just about showing them what they've seen before; it's about anticipating their needs and preferences based on their broader engagement across your brand portfolio.

We achieve this by tracking user events and translating that behavior into affinities [5]. These affinities are then used to re-rank search results, ensuring that what a user sees is not just a generic list, but a curated selection tailored specifically to them [3, 6]. Even for new visitors, Algolia can provide a personalized experience from the get-go by leveraging trends and general user behavior.

## Driving Value Across Brands

The ultimate goal here is to boost **conversion rates** and **customer loyalty** by delivering exceptional, relevant experiences. When users can find what they're looking for quickly and discover new items they'll love, they stick around and buy more. This applies across all your brands, ensuring each touchpoint contributes to your overall business objectives.

sources.chunk_text: Imported from Authority Golden Ledger

question: What successes does Algolia have in fashion and e-commerce, with proof?
answer:
You know how some fashion brands just *get* it, while others leave you feeling like you're lost in a giant closet? We're the ones who make sure your customers find exactly what they're looking for, faster than a runway model changes outfits.

## Algolia's Fashion & E-commerce Wins

Algolia is a powerhouse in the fashion and e-commerce space, driving serious business impact for brands looking to elevate their customer experience and, ultimately, their bottom line. We've got a proven track record of helping these businesses convert more browsers into buyers.

Check out how we've helped:

* **Oh Polly**: This fast-growing online fashion brand struggled with a clunky search experience that led to negative customer feedback. After implementing Algolia, they saw **faster, more relevant search results**, which directly translated to improved **Click-Through Rates (CTR)** and **Conversion Rates (CVR)**. The kicker? Search sessions became responsible for a massive **20% of their total revenue**. [Source: Oh-Polly Case Study](https://www.algolia.com/customers/Oh-Polly)

* **END. Clothing**: A global retailer focused on fashion, sneakers, and streetwear, END. wanted to improve their community-oriented customer journey. By deploying Algolia for **Search, Browse, and Recommendations**, they’ve achieved a **2% increase in conversions** and significantly reduced their merchandising workload through **Dynamic Re-ranking**. They're seeing improved CTRs on their product listing pages and are excited about the potential for AI-driven recommendations to further boost their business. [Source: END. Clothing Success Story](https://www.algolia.com/customers/END.)

* **Intelligent Fashion Solution**: We've developed AI-powered capabilities specifically for online fashion retail. This solution understands style nuances, enabling features like **Automated Merchandising** and **Style-Aware Retrieval**, ensuring that fashion-forward customers find exactly what they're looking for, even if they don't know the exact keywords. [Source: Intelligent Fashion Solution](https://www.algolia.com/about/news/algolia-redefines-online-fashion-retail-with-the-next-generation-of-its-intelligent-fashion-solution)

These examples showcase how Algolia doesn't just provide search; we deliver **business-driving solutions** that directly impact revenue and customer satisfaction in the competitive fashion and e-commerce landscape.

Are you involved in selecting search solutions, or are you evaluating them for a specific team or project?
